interpretation_summary: The replication followed the preregistered plan to test whether WTP is lower under a lottery frame than a gift frame among participants passing a comprehension check. Execution succeeded after resolving a minor dtype error. The main analysis (check==3) found the expected direction (gift > lottery) but was not statistically significant (p=0.143; Hedges g≈0.22) and showed extreme outliers in the gift condition. A preregistered sensitivity analysis restricting WTP<=20 reversed the direction (gift < lottery) and was also non-significant (p=0.128; Hedges g≈-0.25). Compared to the original Experiment 1 (gift > lottery, p<0.001), the replication did not reproduce a significant effect and exhibited instability across specifications, likely due to hypothetical responses and extreme outliers.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment: Implemented the planned Welch t-test comparing WTP across gift vs lottery among comprehension-pass participants (check==3), computed Hedges g, and ran the preregistered sensitivity subset (WTP<=20). Data cleaning of currency-like strings and construction of condition variable matched the plan. The overall design mirrors the original Experiment 1 in structure but uses a single WTP task with hypothetical valuations.

deviations.issue_description: Valuation task appears hypothetical rather than incentive-compatible as in the original Experiment 1, and includes extreme outliers (e.g., WTP up to 7000) in the gift condition.
deviations.impact: High: Inflated variance, unstable estimates, and sensitivity direction reversal likely attenuated power and distorted effect estimates.

deviations.issue_description: Minor code adjustment to handle dtype promotion error when constructing the condition variable.
deviations.impact: Low: Did not alter analysis logic; resolved execution to match preregistration.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Among participants who correctly identify the lowest possible outcome, mean WTP for a 50/50 $10/$20 prospect framed as a lottery ticket is lower than mean WTP for the same prospect framed as an uncertain gift certificate (WTP_lottery < WTP_gift).
results_comparison.original_results: Experiment 1 (original): WTP_lottery M=$5.68 vs WTP_gift M=$9.52; t(99)=3.43, p<0.001; direction: gift > lottery (significant).
results_comparison.replication_results: Main (check==3): gift n=86, mean=186.22, sd=1059.24; lottery n=82, mean=17.28, sd=16.39; Welch t=-1.479, p=0.1429; Hedges g=0.222; direction: gift > lottery (non-significant). Sensitivity (check==3 & WTP<=20): gift n=76, mean=10.99, sd=6.34; lottery n=71, mean=12.71, sd=7.23; Welch t=1.529, p=0.1285; Hedges g=-0.252; direction: gift < lottery (non-significant).
results_comparison.overall_answer: No. The replication did not meet the preregistered criterion of a statistically significant effect in the expected direction. The main analysis showed the expected direction but was not significant; the sensitivity analysis reversed direction and was also not significant.

replication_report: Replication not successful: The hypothesized framing effect on WTP (gift > lottery) was not statistically significant and was unstable across specifications, contrasting with the original significant effect.

failure_handling.suggestions: Preempt dtype issues by explicitly casting variables and avoiding nested np.where with mixed types; add unit tests for data cleaning; ensure consistent string-to-numeric conversion before condition construction.

Replicate incentive-compatible payoffs to curb unrealistic entries; preregister robust handling of extreme values (e.g., range checks, verification prompts, winsorization, trimmed means); consider median-based tests or nonparametric alternatives; pre-validate input ranges within survey.

If incentive-compatibility cannot be implemented, preregister and justify alternative robustness strategies (e.g., log-transformations, capped ranges) and collect ancillary measures (attention, comprehension, belief elicitation) to diagnose outlier behavior.
Report confidence intervals and graphical diagnostics (histograms/boxplots) to convey distributional issues; supplement with permutation or bootstrap tests robust to skew/outliers.

notes: Extreme heteroskedasticity and outliers (especially in the gift condition) likely undermined power and stability. Results may be sensitive to incentive compatibility and response validation. Future attempts should use real payoffs, tighter input validation, and pre-registered robust estimators to better match the original context.
